_comment: Machine-readable rule set for bin/policy-check.js and adapters/opencode/plugin.ts. Kept as flat JSON so it can be parsed with jq or any language's built-in JSON support, no extra dependencies. Rule patterns adapted from roboticforce/agent-guardrails, CodyLunders/claude-code-hooks-library, kornysietsma/claude-code-permissions-hook, and AperionAI/shield (all MIT/Apache-2.0) -- not written from scratch. IMPORTANT LIMITATION (confirmed by four independent external audits, July 2026): regex matching on the final command string cannot see through shell preprocessing -- quote removal, $IFS word-splitting tricks, parameter expansion slicing, and multi-layer obfuscation can all defeat a pattern match in principle. The 'obfuscation' category below raises the bar against the most common/cheap evasion techniques (subshells hiding a keyword, IFS manipulation, eval, encoded-blob-to-shell) but this is a best-effort mitigation, not a proof of coverage. This limitation is inherent to regex-based interception as a category, not unique to this implementation -- see tests/policy-cases.json for the adversarial cases this is verified against. The 'scope' field on each rule controls which extracted-string origins it's tested against: 'bash' rules only run against Bash commands, never against Edit/Write/MultiEdit file content -- without this, editing this very policy engine's own test fixture (which contains literal strings like "rm -rf node_modules" as JSON data) would trip the destructive_ops rules and block the edit. 'any' is for rules that should also catch a secret accidentally pasted into a file, not just typed as a command. The 'keywords' field (optional) is a cheap pre-filter: evaluate() only runs the full regex against a string if at least one keyword is a case-insensitive substring of it. Every keyword listed MUST be a literal substring guaranteed present in any real match of that rule's pattern -- keywords only ever widen what gets a full regex check, never narrow it, so a wrong/missing keyword can make matching slower but must never cause a false negative. When adding or editing a rule with keywords, re-verify that guarantee by hand against the pattern before relying on it, and add a regression case to tests/policy-cases.json.

rules.message: This deletes files/folders permanently with no undo. What exactly is being deleted and why?
Force-push can overwrite remote history other people (or your future self) depend on. Use --force-with-lease if you're sure, or explain why this is safe.
This discards uncommitted work with no undo. Confirm nothing important is uncommitted first.
Force-deleting a branch discards its commits if unmerged.
This permanently deletes a database table/schema and its data. This should almost never run outside a migration you wrote deliberately.
This UPDATE/DELETE has no WHERE clause — it will affect every row in the table. Is that intended?
This tears down real infrastructure.
This deletes a namespace or applies a broad delete manifest.
777 makes files world-writable — usually not what you want, even to unblock something quickly.
This would print secret values into the chat log. Consider checking a specific key with grep instead of dumping the whole file.
.env files hold secrets and should never be committed. Add it to .gitignore instead.
This reads environment/secret values and sends them to a network destination in the same command — blocked pending explicit confirmation of what's being sent where.

This mentions something tagged production alongside a destructive-sounding word. Confirm explicitly this is actually a destructive action against production, not e.g. a commit message or unrelated command — this check is a soft keyword match, not a precise one.
A live Stripe secret key is present. Live-mode Stripe actions move real money — confirm this is intentional, not a copy-paste from test mode.
This provisions billed cloud infrastructure. Confirm you understand the ongoing cost before this runs.
Switching Stripe to live mode means real charges to real cards. Confirm this is intentional.

This hides a destructive-sounding command inside a subshell ($(...) or backticks), which is a common way to sneak a dangerous action past a simple keyword check. What does this subshell actually produce?

This manipulates IFS (the shell's word-separator variable), which is an unusual, rarely-legitimate way to split a command that can be used to hide its real meaning from simple pattern checks. What is this command actually doing?
eval executes a dynamically-built string as a command, which this policy engine can't inspect ahead of time. What command does this actually run?

This decodes an encoded blob and pipes it straight into a shell interpreter, which is a well-known way to run a hidden command that no simple keyword check can read in advance. What does the decoded content actually contain?
